nodes.summary: Sets the deck's scope: the issue is no longer only faster typing or inline code completion, but a team operating model for steering agents across an end-to-end engineering lifecycle.
nodes.title: Slide 01 - Building the AI-Native Engineering Team

nodes.summary: Explains why coding assistants should now be discussed as agents: longer task horizons make planning, tool use, verification, and handoff part of the product.
nodes.title: Slide 02 - The Multi-Hour Milestone Has Been Breached

nodes.summary: Makes the core talk distinction visible: coding assistants are shifting from suggesting code inside an editor to operating across tools, context, tests, and review loops.
nodes.title: Slide 03 - From Inline Suggestions To Autonomous Workflows

nodes.summary: Turns the term agent into an inspectable architecture: broad context, retained state, tool execution, and measurable evaluation are what make agentic workflows different from chat prompts.
nodes.title: Slide 04 - The Architectural Pillars Of Coding Agents

nodes.summary: Introduces the control pattern for the rest of the deck: agents can take the first pass, but engineers still review output and own strategy, risk, and final decisions.
nodes.title: Slide 05 - A New Operating System For Engineering Management

nodes.summary: Shows planning as the first controlled agent workflow: use agents to analyse and structure work, but keep prioritisation and sequencing with people.
nodes.title: Slide 06 - Phase 01: Plan

nodes.summary: Shows agents as design translators, not design owners: they can scaffold from mockups and apply tokens, while humans retain UX and system-level judgement.
nodes.title: Slide 07 - Phase 02: Design

nodes.summary: Shows the strongest productivity promise: turning clear plans and specs into working code, while validating command execution and test feedback.
nodes.title: Slide 08 - Phase 03: Build

nodes.summary: Makes validation the governing mechanism for autonomous builds. The agent can help write and maintain tests, but humans own adversarial thinking and test intent.
nodes.title: Slide 09 - Phase 04: Test

nodes.summary: Positions agents as review amplifiers: useful for broad tracing and defect hints, but not a replacement for final merge authority or production-readiness judgement.
nodes.title: Slide 10 - Phase 05: Review

nodes.summary: Shows documentation as part of the delivery loop rather than an afterthought: agents can draft evidence and diagrams, while humans retain public, critical, and brand-sensitive documentation decisions.
nodes.title: Slide 11 - Phase 06: Document

nodes.summary: Draws the boundary for operational use: agents can help triage logs and propose remediation, but production incidents and sensitive changes need human sign-off.
nodes.title: Slide 12 - Phase 07: Deploy And Maintain

nodes.summary: Provides the deck's practical governance artefact: a team can decide, phase by phase, what AI leads, what is shared, and what humans must own.
nodes.title: Slide 13 - Responsibility Matrix

nodes.summary: Shows the higher-level productivity argument: the return is not just faster phases, but a connected workflow where each stage creates context for the next.
nodes.title: Slide 14 - The AI-Native Flywheel

nodes.summary: Turns the deck into an action plan: write repo rules, connect bounded context, and enforce validation loops before expanding agent authority.
nodes.title: Slide 15 - Integration Architecture Rollout

nodes.summary: This page is the end-to-end demonstration route for Challenge 2. It shows how the wiki, Obsidian vault, SeeLinks-style workbench, browser AI export, benchmark, harness, and audit trail fit the challenge brief: make government "dark data" findable, structured, source-backed, and reviewable.
nodes.title: Challenge 2 Demonstration Guide

nodes.summary: This benchmark is documentation only. It is designed to test how well an AI coding agent can use the Challenge 2 wiki as a source-backed knowledge base, not how much it already knows about government policy.

nodes.summary: Dark Data Workbench is the browser interface for the Challenge 2 knowledge base. It sits alongside the Obsidian vault and uses the generated source register and wiki notes as its data source.

nodes.summary: Build **Dark Data Workbench** as a self-contained SvelteKit app inside the Challenge 2 repo. It will let users browse the generated Obsidian/wiki corpus with or without AI: deterministic search/filter/context-building first, then optional browser-AI export and local MCP access to the same selected evidence set. The v1 implementation will not require a model API. It will preserve synthetic data as-is, keep raw Challenge 2 source files read-only, and make the workbench prominent from the Obsidian knowledge base index.

nodes.summary: Implement the approved **Dark Data Workbench** plan on a new branch named `codex/SeeLinks`. The branch will add a self-contained SvelteKit workbench under `challenge-2/workbench/`, expose optional browser-AI and MCP context flows, and add a test suite with Playwright coverage at least comparable to SeeLinks. Coverage enforcement will be available through an explicit optional command, not the default test path.

nodes.summary: This case study turns the AI Engineering Lab Challenge 2 build into talk material for a mixed HMRC audience. It is not a claim that Codex built or assured a production government service. It is evidence that a coding agent can accelerate a bounded, synthetic-data engineering prototype when the human owner supplies intent, constraints, review, and validation.

nodes.summary: This operating model is designed for public-sector teams that want to try coding agents without pretending they are autonomous engineers, security authorities, or policy owners. It combines the repo-local Challenge 2 evidence with government AI, security, service, and data-protection guidance.
nodes.title: Operating Model For Public-Sector Engineering With Coding Agents

nodes.title: From Typing Code To Steering Agents

nodes.summary: 1. Start with dark data: guidance exists, but it is buried across documents and is hard to find, structure, trust, and reuse. 2. Explain the workflow shift: engineers move from typing every line to framing intent, supplying context, constraining behaviour, reviewing diffs, and validating evidence. 3. Use Challenge 2 as the concrete case study: the generated wiki, Dark Data Workbench, source cards, evidence bundle, MCP tooling, and evaluation harness make provenance visible. 4. Show where agents help: exploration, scaffolding, refactoring, tests, documentation, report generation, source packaging, and validation support. 5. Draw the boundary: agents do not own security sign-off, production op
nodes.title: Narrative Arc

nodes.summary: Use the market categories as the mixed-audience explanation: IDE autocomplete/chat, repo or terminal agents, PR/review assistants, autonomous issue-to-PR agents, and enterprise control layers. Use the evidence synthesis to avoid a blanket productivity claim: gains are conditional on task shape, repo maturity, user skill, and review burden. Use the public-sector controls to connect tool capability to HMRC-safe adoption: approved data classes, sandboxing, approvals, branch protection, logs, tests, security scanning, and accountable ownership. Use the Q&A section as preparation for questions about tool choice, sensitive data, terminal access, repo instruction files, pilot metrics, and naming.

items.info_text: Sets the deck's scope: the issue is no longer only faster typing or inline code completion, but a team operating model for steering agents across an end-to-end engineering lifecycle.
items.name: Slide 01 - Building the AI-Native Engineering Team

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Explains why coding assistants should now be discussed as agents: longer task horizons make planning, tool use, verification, and handoff part of the product.
items.name: Slide 02 - The Multi-Hour Milestone Has Been Breached

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Makes the core talk distinction visible: coding assistants are shifting from suggesting code inside an editor to operating across tools, context, tests, and review loops.
items.name: Slide 03 - From Inline Suggestions To Autonomous Workflows

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Turns the term agent into an inspectable architecture: broad context, retained state, tool execution, and measurable evaluation are what make agentic workflows different from chat prompts.
items.name: Slide 04 - The Architectural Pillars Of Coding Agents

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Introduces the control pattern for the rest of the deck: agents can take the first pass, but engineers still review output and own strategy, risk, and final decisions.
items.name: Slide 05 - A New Operating System For Engineering Management

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Shows planning as the first controlled agent workflow: use agents to analyse and structure work, but keep prioritisation and sequencing with people.
items.name: Slide 06 - Phase 01: Plan

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Shows agents as design translators, not design owners: they can scaffold from mockups and apply tokens, while humans retain UX and system-level judgement.
items.name: Slide 07 - Phase 02: Design

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Shows the strongest productivity promise: turning clear plans and specs into working code, while validating command execution and test feedback.
items.name: Slide 08 - Phase 03: Build

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Makes validation the governing mechanism for autonomous builds. The agent can help write and maintain tests, but humans own adversarial thinking and test intent.
items.name: Slide 09 - Phase 04: Test

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Positions agents as review amplifiers: useful for broad tracing and defect hints, but not a replacement for final merge authority or production-readiness judgement.
items.name: Slide 10 - Phase 05: Review

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Shows documentation as part of the delivery loop rather than an afterthought: agents can draft evidence and diagrams, while humans retain public, critical, and brand-sensitive documentation decisions.
items.name: Slide 11 - Phase 06: Document

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Draws the boundary for operational use: agents can help triage logs and propose remediation, but production incidents and sensitive changes need human sign-off.
items.name: Slide 12 - Phase 07: Deploy And Maintain

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Provides the deck's practical governance artefact: a team can decide, phase by phase, what AI leads, what is shared, and what humans must own.
items.name: Slide 13 - Responsibility Matrix

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Shows the higher-level productivity argument: the return is not just faster phases, but a connected workflow where each stage creates context for the next.
items.name: Slide 14 - The AI-Native Flywheel

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: Turns the deck into an action plan: write repo rules, connect bounded context, and enforce validation loops before expanding agent authority.
items.name: Slide 15 - Integration Architecture Rollout

properties.publication_status: publishable derived thumbnail and text sidecar; raw imported PowerPoint remains local

items.info_text: This page explains the knowledge-base architecture for someone seeing it for the first time. The short version: the original documents stay untouched, a repeatable builder reads them, and the generated wiki turns them into linked notes that are easy to browse, search, review, and demo.
items.name: Challenge 2 Architecture

items.info_text: This page is the end-to-end demonstration route for Challenge 2. It shows how the wiki, Obsidian vault, SeeLinks-style workbench, browser AI export, benchmark, harness, and audit trail fit the challenge brief: make government "dark data" findable, structured, source-backed, and reviewable.
items.name: Challenge 2 Demonstration Guide

items.info_text: This benchmark is documentation only. It is designed to test how well an AI coding agent can use the Challenge 2 wiki as a source-backed knowledge base, not how much it already knows about government policy.
items.name: Challenge 2 Wiki Evaluation Benchmark

items.info_text: This is the navigation entrypoint for the Challenge 2 Obsidian knowledge base. Raw documents remain in `structured_files/` and `unstructured_files/`; generated knowledge lives under `wiki/`.
items.name: Challenge 2 Knowledge Base Index

items.info_text: Dark Data Workbench is the browser interface for the Challenge 2 knowledge base. It sits alongside the Obsidian vault and uses the generated source register and wiki notes as its data source.
items.name: Dark Data Workbench

items.info_text: Build Challenge 2 as a Karpathy-style LLM Wiki: raw source documents stay immutable, an LLM-maintained Markdown wiki becomes the navigable knowledge layer, and schema/index/log files keep the system disciplined. This follows the pattern in Karpathy’s LLM Wiki gist: raw sources, generated wiki, and explicit operating rules. Use all Challenge 2 documents: 20 structured files and 23 unstructured files from challenge-2. “Translate” means format translation into Obsidian-friendly Markdown, not natural-language translation.

items.info_text: Build **Dark Data Workbench** as a self-contained SvelteKit app inside the Challenge 2 repo. It will let users browse the generated Obsidian/wiki corpus with or without AI: deterministic search/filter/context-building first, then optional browser-AI export and local MCP access to the same selected evidence set. The v1 implementation will not require a model API. It will preserve synthetic data as-is, keep raw Challenge 2 source files read-only, and make the workbench prominent from the Obsidian knowledge base index.

The branch will add a self-contained SvelteKit workbench under `challenge-2/workbench/`, expose optional browser-AI and MCP context flows, and add a test suite with Playwright coverage at least comparable to SeeLinks. Coverage enforcement will be available through an explicit optional command, not the default test path.

items.info_text: ## Core position
items.name: Security, governance and public-sector controls

items.info_text: This case study turns the AI Engineering Lab Challenge 2 build into talk material for a mixed HMRC audience. It is not a claim that Codex built or assured a production government service. It is evidence that a coding agent can accelerate a bounded, synthetic-data engineering prototype when the human owner supplies intent, constraints, review, and validation.
items.name: Repo Case Study: Codex Build

items.info_text: This operating model is designed for public-sector teams that want to try coding agents without pretending they are autonomous engineers, security authorities, or policy owners. It combines the repo-local Challenge 2 evidence with government AI, security, service, and data-protection guidance.
items.name: Operating Model For Public-Sector Engineering With Coding Agents
items.short_name: Operating Model For Public-Sector Engineering With Coding Agents

items.info_text: 1. Start with dark data: guidance exists, but it is buried across documents and is hard to find, structure, trust, and reuse. 2. Explain the workflow shift: engineers move from typing every line to framing intent, supplying context, constraining behaviour, reviewing diffs, and validating evidence. 3. Use Challenge 2 as the concrete case study: the generated wiki, Dark Data Workbench, source cards, evidence bundle, MCP tooling, and evaluation harness make provenance visible. 4. Show where agents help: exploration, scaffolding, refactoring, tests, documentation, report generation, source packaging, and validation support. 5. Draw the boundary: agents do not own security sign-off, production op
items.name: Narrative Arc

items.info_text: Use the market categories as the mixed-audience explanation: IDE autocomplete/chat, repo or terminal agents, PR/review assistants, autonomous issue-to-PR agents, and enterprise control layers. Use the evidence synthesis to avoid a blanket productivity claim: gains are conditional on task shape, repo maturity, user skill, and review burden. Use the public-sector controls to connect tool capability to HMRC-safe adoption: approved data classes, sandboxing, approvals, branch protection, logs, tests, security scanning, and accountable ownership. Use the Q&A section as preparation for questions about tool choice, sensitive data, terminal access, repo instruction files, pilot metrics, and naming.
items.name: AI Coding Assistants Market Briefing

items.info_text: Use this as a short "where agents are heading" sidebar in the HMRC talk. The useful point is not a product prediction; it is the enterprise pattern: persistent agents need identity, permissions, channels, observability, revocation, and evidence. The local brief's most important distinction is that "ClawPilot" can refer to Microsoft's internal Project Lobster work and also to unrelated public/commercial Clawpilot services. Do not collapse those into one product claim.
